screens.title: Indices are shorthand for repeated multiplication
screens.body: For example, x³ means x × x × x. The index tells you how many times the base is multiplied by itself.
screens.keyPoint: When multiplying powers with the same base, add the indices.

screens.title: Use the index laws in order

steps.title: Look for matching bases
steps.body: Index laws only combine powers when their bases are the same.

screens.question: When multiplying powers with the same base, you add the indices.
screens.incorrectFeedback: For multiplication with the same base, add the indices: x² × x⁵ = x⁷.

screens.title: You have the first index law down.
screens.body: That is one of the core tools you will use throughout algebra. Next, you will see what changes when you divide powers.

screens.title: The discriminant tells us about the roots.
screens.body: The expression b² − 4ac determines how many real roots a quadratic has.
